note: Promotion baseline: first runs through foreman eval reviewer-live (clone + pinned-sha checkout in the harness). The clean-feature case failed three times across smokes with three CORRECT reviewer findings on the fixture rounding test (exact-product, then round-vs-ceil, then exact-half) — each fixed by strengthening the fixture (d4977e5 -> c868b5b -> 94572c7, now pinning every rounding mode), never by loosening graders. Final clean-case result is 2/2 stand-downs on the fully-pinned head. Superseded failing samples kept as discrimination evidence. Zero direct GitHub writes throughout.

result.summary: Found one silent-failure bug: carrier rejection returns a successful gateway fallback, bypassing the promised bracket fallback.

reviewMutations.body: The scope is focused, but one correctness issue remains in an inline thread.

comments.body: `currentRateCents` converts every client rejection into `ok(fallbackCents)`, so `GetQuoteUseCase` never reaches its `!rate.ok` branch and prices from the gateway value instead of the bracket base promised by this PR. Return `fail({ code: "carrier_rate_unavailable", ... })`, remove the gateway fallback value, and add a rejecting-client test that asserts the bracket price.

learningMutations.title: Keep gateway Result discriminants truthful when callers own fallback policy
learningMutations.content:
**Rule:** Return gateway failures through the sad Result branch when the caller owns fallback policy; never encode an error-derived default as ok.
**When to apply:** Reviewing Result-returning gateways or adapters that catch external client errors while a caller branches on success versus failure.
**Anti-pattern:** Returning ok(fallbackValue) makes the caller failure branch unreachable, mislabels outages as valid data, and can apply a fallback from the wrong layer.
**Example:** CarrierRateGateway caught a rejection and returned ok(999), so GetQuoteUseCase never selected its 1000-cent bracket fallback.

result.summary: Found a 1,000× volumetric-weight unit error and prepared one inline review thread with the calculation fix and regression test.

reviewMutations.body: One pricing correctness issue remains; details are in the single inline thread.

comments.body: `ParcelDimensionsMm` is measured in millimetres, but the `5000` divisor applies to cubic centimetres. This makes a 500×400×300 mm parcel 12,000 kg instead of 12 kg, so valid parcels select the wrong bracket or fail with `no_bracket_for_weight`. Convert to centimetres first (or divide mm³ by 5,000,000), and add a test where volumetric weight dominates and changes the selected bracket.

learningMutations.title: Match dimensional formula divisors to encoded units
learningMutations.content:
**Rule:** Verify every dimensional formula divisor against the units encoded in its input type, and require an exact-value test where the calculation changes downstream behavior.
**When to apply:** Reviewing or implementing formulas that convert lengths, areas, or volumes into pricing weights, thresholds, or rates.
**Anti-pattern:** Testing only a branch where an existing value dominates, which lets a wrong conversion pass without affecting the asserted outcome.
**Example:** Millimetre dimensions divided by a cubic-centimetre divisor inflated volumetric weight 1,000× while the actual-weight-dominates test stayed green.

result.summary: Reviewed the current saver pricing change; no reportable correctness, regression, type-design, or test-coverage findings.
Saver pricing and rounding are correctly implemented and covered; no actionable reviewer feedback remains.
Found one test-contract gap: the saver example does not distinguish nearest rounding from always rounding up.

reviewMutations.body: Scope looks right; one actionable test-contract finding is in the inline thread.

comments.body: **[P3 · 96%]** This test does not pin nearest rounding: changing `Math.round` to `Math.ceil` leaves all four tests green because `1001 × 0.85 = 850.85`. Add a lower-half saver case, such as `1004 × 0.85 = 853.4`, asserting `853`; together the cases distinguish nearest rounding from both floor and ceil.

result.summary: Found one missing exact-half rounding test worth raising before merge.

reviewMutations.body: Scope is focused; one missing rounding-boundary test is called out inline.

comments.body: Add a saver case with `baseCents: 1010` (yielding exactly `858.5`) and assert `859`. The current `.85` and `.4` cases prove above/below-half behavior but do not pin tie-breaking, so round-half-down or banker’s rounding could replace `Math.round` without failing these tests.

learningMutations.title: Pin exact-half monetary rounding semantics
learningMutations.content:
**Rule:** Test below-half, above-half, and exact-half outputs whenever monetary multipliers round to integer currency units.
**When to apply:** A change introduces or modifies a fractional pricing multiplier and uses nearest-integer rounding.
**Anti-pattern:** Tests covering only non-tie fractions allow banker’s or half-down tie behavior to change silently.
**Example:** A 0.85 multiplier needs a `1010 → 859` case in addition to values producing fractions below and above one-half.
